aliases: Fried Tofu and Tempeh with Sambal

english: Fried Tofu and Tempeh with Sambal

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Tahu Tempe Penyet. Closest parent is tempeh-based food product (FOODON_03302050); tofu and sambal components are not individually coded in this mapping.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Tempeh) used as closest parent. No composite code exists for Tahu Tempe Penyet as a combined dish with sambal.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Indonesian soy dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 g, comprising one piece of fried tofu (~80 g), one piece of fried tempeh (~80 g), and a portion of sambal (~90 g), as typically served in Singaporean Indonesian-style eateries.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient composition estimated from component foods: Fried Tofu (Tahu Goreng) and Fried Tempeh (Tempe Goreng) with Sambal, using FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as supporting references
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table (2020); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Tahu Tempe Penyet as a composite dish. Values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data for fried tofu (~120 kcal/100g, protein ~10g, fat ~7g, carb ~3g) and fried tempeh (~200 kcal/100g, protein ~14g, fat ~11g, carb ~10g), weighted at approximately 40% tofu, 40% tempeh, 20% sambal. Sambal contribution estimated from USDA SR Legacy chilli paste data. Weighted composite per 100g of the combined dish is used. Values are moderate-confidence estimates.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soybean-based foods including tofu and tempeh; analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for tofu and tempeh
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for Tahu Tempe Penyet as a composite dish. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tofu (GI ~15) and tempeh (GI ~41), weighted at 50:50 for the soy protein components. Sambal contribution is negligible to glycemic load due to very low carbohydrate content. Composite GI of ~30 is a defensible low-GI estimate. GL per serving = (30 × 8.5 × 250/100) / 100 = 6.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from sambal and frying process
hypertension.guidance: Tahu Tempe Penyet contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100g, contributing ~800 mg per standard serving; individuals with hypertension should request reduced sambal or consume smaller portions to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index from soy protein base
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 30 and GL of 6.4 per serving, Tahu Tempe Penyet is a low-glycemic dish suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as white rice; the high protein and fibre content from tofu and tempeh further support postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Soy isoflavones and unsaturated fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tofu and tempeh provide soy isoflavones and a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio (~4:1), which may support LDL cholesterol reduction; however, the deep-frying method and sambal sodium content warrant moderation for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tahu Tempe Penyet is a popular Indonesian-origin dish widely served in Singapore, consisting of deep-fried firm tofu (tahu) and deep-fried tempeh (tempe) that are smashed or pressed flat and served with a spicy sambal chilli paste. The dish originates from Javanese cuisine, where 'penyet' means 'smashed' in Javanese, referring to the technique of pressing the fried protein against a mortar to create a rustic, slightly crushed texture that allows the sambal to penetrate the surface. The tofu provides a soft, silken interior with a crispy fried exterior, while the tempeh contributes a firm, nutty, and slightly earthy flavour due to its fermentation process. The sambal, typically made from ground red chillies, shrimp paste (belacan), garlic, and lime, adds intense heat and umami depth. Nutritionally, the dish is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-glycemic meal. Per 100g, it provides approximately 162 kcal, 10.8g protein, 9.6g fat, and 8.5g carbohydrate, with a glycemic index of approximately 30. The soy base contributes isoflavones, calcium, iron, and dietary fibre. Sodium content is moderate to high due to the sambal and frying process, making portion control relevant for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, halal, and dairy-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary preferences in Singapore's multicultural food landscape. It is commonly served as a main dish alongside steamed white rice, lontong (compressed rice cake), or as a standalone protein dish.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tahu tempe penyet?
Is tahu tempe penyet suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of tahu tempe penyet?
How much protein in tahu tempe penyet?
What is the sodium content of tahu tempe penyet?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Tahu Tempe Penyet as a composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component data (fried tofu, fried tempeh, sambal) using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for tofu and tempeh. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates; no single approved-source composite entry was found for Tahu Tempe Penyet. Values should be updated if HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA publishes a direct composite entry. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA component data used. 4. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA component data used. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for these soy-based fried foods. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA component data used. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA component data used. 8. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as tofu and tempeh fried in vegetable oil do not contribute measurable trans fat per FAO/INFOODS SEA data. 9. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as tofu and tempeh are plant-based soy foods containing no dietary cholesterol. 10. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for tofu and tempeh. 11. GI estimated at 30 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for tofu and tempeh; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found. 12. Dietary flags: is_gluten_free set to false as tempeh may be processed with wheat-containing substrates in some preparations; consumers with coeliac disease should verify. 13. Sambal may contain shrimp paste (belacan) in non-vegetarian versions; the vegetarian/vegan flag applies to the standard preparation without belacan as served in vegetarian-certified outlets.
